updating_rows.label: Updating Rows
updating_rows.summary: Learn what `UPDATE` does, how to change one column or multiple columns, and why `WHERE` is critical when modifying data.

sketch1.title: How do you change one column?

sketch2.title: How do you change multiple columns?

what_update_does.title: `UPDATE` changes values in rows that already exist.
what_update_does.bodyMarkdown:
So far, you have read data and added data.

`UPDATE` is different.
It changes data that is already stored in the table.

That means SQL can modify an existing row instead of creating a new one.

So the big idea is:

> `UPDATE` changes existing rows in a table

changing_one_column.title: A simple update can change one column in matching rows.
changing_one_column.bodyMarkdown:
A beginner update often changes just one value, such as:

- a price
- a status
- a date

This is useful when one part of the row needs to be corrected or refreshed.

So the key idea is:

> one update can target one column in selected rows

changing_multiple_columns.title: One update statement can change more than one column at once.
changing_multiple_columns.bodyMarkdown:
Sometimes a row needs several changes together.

For example, you might update:

- the `status`
- and the `last_updated`
- and maybe another column too

That helps keep related changes together.

So the main idea is:

> one `UPDATE` can modify multiple columns in the same matched rows

why_where_is_critical.title: `WHERE` is critical because it controls which rows get changed.
why_where_is_critical.bodyMarkdown:
This is one of the most important data-change ideas in SQL.

Without `WHERE`, an update can affect **every row** in the table.

That is why update safety starts with asking:

- which rows should change?
- how will SQL know which ones I mean?

So the important idea is:

> `WHERE` decides which rows are changed, and missing it can be dangerous

m12_s01_update_one_price.title: Change one column
m12_s01_update_one_price.prompt:
The SQLite workspace already has the `inventory_ops` dataset loaded.

Write a query that updates `price` to `16.50` for the row where `id` = 5 in `inventory_items`.

Then click **Run**.

help.concept: A simple update changes one column in selected rows.
help.hint_1: Use `UPDATE inventory_items`, set `price`, and target the row with a `WHERE` condition.
help.hint_2: Build a query that changes only the `price` column and uses `id = 5` to limit the update to one row.

m12_s01_update_one_price.hint: Use SET for `price` and a WHERE condition on `id`.
m12_s01_update_one_price.starterCode: -- Update the price for one row

m12_s01_update_one_status.title: Change another single column
m12_s01_update_one_status.prompt:
The tables already exist.

Write a query that updates `status` to `'active'` for the row where `id` = 2 in `inventory_items`.

Then run it.

help.concept: One-column updates are a common beginner way to correct or refresh stored data.
help.hint_1: Change only the `status` column and target the row with `id = 2`.
help.hint_2: Build an update that sets one column value and uses a WHERE condition so only one row is changed.

m12_s01_update_one_status.starterCode: -- Update the status for one row

m12_s01_update_multiple_columns.title: Change multiple columns
m12_s01_update_multiple_columns.prompt:
The tables already exist.

Write a query that updates the row where `id` = 6 so that:

- `status` becomes `'inactive'`
- `last_updated` becomes `'2026-02-10'`

Then click **Run**.

help.concept: One update statement can change multiple columns in the same matched row.
help.hint_1: Use one `UPDATE` statement with more than one assignment in the `SET` clause.
help.hint_2: Build a query that updates both `status` and `last_updated`, then use `WHERE id = 6` so only that row changes.

m12_s01_update_multiple_columns.hint: Use one SET clause with two assignments.
m12_s01_update_multiple_columns.starterCode: -- Update two columns in one row

m12_s01_preview_before_update.title: See why WHERE matters
m12_s01_preview_before_update.prompt:
The tables already exist.

Write a query that returns all columns from `inventory_items` where `id` = 3.

Then run it and inspect the row that would be affected by a future update.

help.concept: Previewing one target row helps learners see why WHERE should point clearly at the intended row.
help.hint_1: Use a normal SELECT query with a WHERE condition on `id`.
help.hint_2: Build a query that reads the full row for one item so you can see exactly what row a future update should target.

m12_s01_preview_before_update.hint: Use SELECT * with a WHERE condition on id.
m12_s01_preview_before_update.starterCode: -- Preview one row before changing it

m12_s01_update_intro_project.title: Practice basic updates
m12_s01_update_intro_project.prompt:
Use the SQL workspace on the right to practice update statements on the `inventory_ops` dataset.

For each task:

1. write the update or preview query carefully
2. check which row or rows should change
3. click **Run**
4. keep your WHERE condition precise

Tasks:

1. Update the price for one row
2. Update the status for one row
3. Update two columns in one row
4. Preview one row before changing it
5. Update `last_updated` for the row where `id` = 1

Run one query at a time.
This project is about learning how to change stored data carefully.

help.concept: Update practice is about changing the intended rows only, with clear SET and WHERE logic.
help.hint_1: Start by deciding exactly which row should change, then write the SET part clearly.
help.hint_2: Solve the tasks one by one. Focus on the WHERE clause first, then write the column changes you want in the SET clause.

m12_s01_update_intro_project.hint: Decide which row should change before writing SET.
m12_s01_update_intro_project.starterCode:
-- Task 1
-- Update the price for one row

m12_s01_update_meaning.title: What does UPDATE do?
m12_s01_update_meaning.prompt: What is the main job of `UPDATE` in SQL?

help.concept: UPDATE changes values in rows that already exist in the table.
help.hint_2: It modifies existing data instead of inserting new rows.

options.a: It changes values in existing rows

m12_s01_one_column_meaning.title: What is a one-column update?
m12_s01_one_column_meaning.prompt: What is the main idea of changing one column with UPDATE?

help.concept: A one-column update changes one selected field in the matched rows.
help.hint_1: Think one field changes, not the whole row structure.
help.hint_2: The query changes one chosen column value for the rows matched by WHERE.

m12_s01_one_column_meaning.hint: Think one chosen column value changes.

options.a: It changes one selected column in the matched rows
options.c: It inserts another copy of the row

m12_s01_multiple_columns_meaning.title: What is a multi-column update?
m12_s01_multiple_columns_meaning.prompt: What is the main idea of changing multiple columns in one update?

help.concept: One UPDATE can make several changes to the same matched rows at once.
help.hint_1: Think one row, several fields updated together.
help.hint_2: This helps keep related changes in one statement.

m12_s01_multiple_columns_meaning.hint: Think one statement can change several columns together.

options.a: One UPDATE can change several columns in the same matched rows
options.b: Each column must always use a separate table
options.c: Multiple columns can only be changed with DELETE
options.d: It means WHERE is no longer needed

m12_s01_where_critical_meaning.title: Why is WHERE critical in updates?
m12_s01_where_critical_meaning.prompt: Why is `WHERE` so important in an update statement?

help.concept: WHERE decides which rows are changed, so it is the main safety control in UPDATE.
help.hint_2: Without a row filter, the update can affect more data than intended.

m12_s01_where_critical_meaning.hint: Think WHERE controls which rows get changed.

options.a: It controls which rows get changed

m12_s01_missing_where_danger.title: What can happen if WHERE is missing in UPDATE?
m12_s01_missing_where_danger.prompt: What is a major danger of forgetting WHERE in an update statement?

help.concept: Without WHERE, UPDATE can affect every row in the table.
help.hint_2: The statement may still run, but it can change far more rows than intended.

m12_s01_missing_where_danger.hint: Think it can change every row.

options.a: It can change every row in the table
options.b: It always adds a new row instead
options.d: It only changes the first row

m12_s01_update_true.title: What is true about UPDATE?
m12_s01_update_true.prompt: Select all true statements about UPDATE.

help.concept: UPDATE modifies existing data using SET and often a WHERE condition.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about modifying existing rows safely.

m12_s01_update_true.hint: Think changing existing rows with control.

options.b: UPDATE uses SET to define the new values
options.c: UPDATE often needs WHERE for safety
options.d: UPDATE only reads data and never changes it
options.e: UPDATE can change one or more columns

m12_s01_one_multi_true.title: What is true about changing one or multiple columns?
m12_s01_one_multi_true.prompt: Select all true statements about one-column and multi-column updates.

help.concept: UPDATE can target one field or several fields in the matched rows.
help.hint_1: Think one field or several fields.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about flexibility inside the SET clause.

m12_s01_one_multi_true.hint: Think SET can describe one or many column changes.

options.a: One update can change just one column
options.b: One update can change multiple columns
options.c: Related changes can be grouped in one statement
options.d: Multiple columns can never be changed together
options.e: The WHERE clause still matters in both cases

m12_s01_where_true.title: What is true about WHERE in updates?
m12_s01_where_true.prompt: Select all true statements about WHERE in update statements.

help.concept: WHERE is the row filter that limits what the update touches.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about narrowing the update to the intended rows.

m12_s01_where_true.hint: Think WHERE decides which rows are touched.

options.a: WHERE limits which rows are updated
options.b: WHERE can help target one row precisely
options.c: WHERE is a major safety tool in UPDATE
options.d: WHERE always makes the update more dangerous
options.e: Missing WHERE can lead to accidental broad changes

m12_s01_update_workflow_true.title: What matches a good beginner workflow for updates?
m12_s01_update_workflow_true.prompt: Select all statements that match a good beginner workflow for writing update statements.

help.concept: A good workflow is to identify the target rows, preview them if needed, then write the SET and WHERE parts carefully.
help.hint_2: Choose the steps that reduce risk when changing stored data.

m12_s01_update_workflow_true.hint: Identify the target rows first, then write SET and WHERE carefully.

options.a: Decide which row or rows should change
options.b: Use WHERE to target them precisely
options.d: Preview the row first when helpful
options.e: Ignore which rows the statement will affect
